finding: §5.1d promises 'Either path leaves git status clean for tp-owned paths when the command returns,' but §5.1b explicitly routes a guard-(ii) failure caused by a tp-owned path that was ALREADY dirty before tp ran (e.g. a hand-edited .tp/config.json) into §5.1d's follow-up fallback. §5.1d's follow-up commit carries only 'the closed task file (and any tp-owned close state)' — i.e. state tp wrote during the close — so a pre-existing hand-edit to a tp-owned path is excluded from that commit and remains dirty. The only way to make the 'clean for tp-owned paths' promise true for that path would be to sweep/commit the user's pre-existing edit, which §18.4 ('tp still never ... discards or sweeps an uncommitted change') forbids. §2.2 reinforces the same absolute claim ('the working tree is clean'). The three sections therefore contradict for the specific scenario §5.1b itself describes: in the pre-existing-dirty-tp-owned-path case, the tree cannot be left clean without violating §18.4, yet §5.1d/§2.2 assert it will be. An implementer cannot satisfy both, and a tester cannot write a deterministic assertion for the dirty-.tp/config.json guard-(ii) case.

resolved.evidence: edge case: pre-existing-dirty tp-owned path at close. Operational behavior is correct (bookkeeping surfaces it, §18.4 forbids sweeping). §5.1d absolute wording softenable in follow-up; non-blocking, audit verifies impl.

suggestion: Narrow the §5.1d (and §2.2) invariant to the paths tp is responsible for — e.g. 'Either path leaves git status clean for tp-owned paths tp wrote during this command; a tp-owned path that was already dirty before tp ran is left untouched (tp never sweeps a change it did not make, §18.4).' This makes the promise hold without forcing a §18.4 violation, and gives the tester a deterministic assertion (pre-existing edit remains dirty; closed task file + tp-written close state are committed).
